title: Outdoor Sofa — Modern Comfort Collection
summary: A full-size outdoor sofa with a real reclined back — the backrest is a separate slatted panel that leans into the frame, so it's comfortable before the cushions even arrive.
description:
Most DIY outdoor sofas are boxes with cushions, and they sit like boxes. This design is different where it counts: the back is built as its own slatted panel that installs LEANING — anchored at the seat base, resting against the frame's top rail — so the recline is real geometry, not two inches of foam pretending. Paired with a slatted seat deck on cleats, the sofa is comfortable in a way box-frame builds aren't.

The frame is pocket-hole joinery throughout, and the plan is disciplined about hiding it: every step names which way the holes face (inside, or down). Follow that — on patio furniture there's no 'wall side' to hide mistakes against.

Two build notes matter. First, the plan's own correction: the diagram in step 6 is wrong, and the back base should be 79-1/2" with 2" overhangs — the cut list is right, the picture isn't. Second, the slat-spacing trick in step 5 (attach ends and middle first, then space the rest) is the difference between an even back and one with a cumulative half-inch error at the far end. Size cushions before building: the 23-1/2" seat slats suit standard 24–25" deep outdoor seat cushions.

tools.note: The whole frame is 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Back rest top and seat slat cleats.

materials.name: Outdoor cushions, seat and back
materials.note: Size before building — the frame suits standard ~24"-deep seat cushions. Not in the cost estimate.

cutList.note: The step-6 diagram shows this wrong — 79-1/2" with 2" overhangs is correct.

steps.title: Legs to arm rests
steps.body: Drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes in the top of each leg and attach to the arm rests from underneath with 2-1/2" pocket screws. The diagrams show the assembly upside down — build it that way, it's easier.

steps.title: Add the 2x4 arm supports
steps.body: Pocket-hole both ends of the 2x4 side supports and attach between the legs, holes facing inside where they'll never be seen.

steps.title: Add the 2x6 end aprons
steps.body: Pocket-hole each 2x6 end apron — two holes per end, plus three along one edge (those face up later, for attaching things above). Install inside the legs, flush to the outside faces, holes inward.

steps.title: Connect the two arms
steps.body: Pocket-hole the long 2x6 bases and the 2x4 back rail, and join the two arm assemblies into the sofa frame — all pocket holes on the insides or facing down. This is the step where the sofa gets big; work on a flat patio, not a cluttered garage floor.

steps.title: Build the back panel — ends and middle first
steps.body:
Screw the 2x2 back-rest top to the nine back slats, two 2-1/2" self-tapping screws per joint.

Use the plan's spacing trick: attach the two END slats and the MIDDLE slat first, then distribute the rest evenly between them. Spacing all nine left-to-right accumulates error; anchoring the ends and middle traps it.

steps.title: Attach the back panel to its base
steps.body:
Screw the slat panel to the 79-1/2" 2x6 back base, keeping the same slat spacing as the top, with a 2" overhang at each end.

Note: the source diagram for this step is wrong — trust the cut list (79-1/2", 2" overhangs), not the picture.

steps.title: Install the back, leaning
steps.body: Set the back panel inside the sofa frame: its base sits on the seat area 8" in from the back edge, screwed down with 2-1/2" screws, and the top LEANS against the frame's back rail. That lean is the recline — don't square it up.

steps.title: Lock the back to the rail
steps.body: Screw through a few of the back slats into the back rail where they touch. The back is now anchored at the bottom and pinned at the top — sit against it hard and check nothing moves.

steps.title: Add the rear seat cleat
steps.body: Screw a 2x2 cleat along the back base 2x6, screws about every 12". This carries the back edge of the seat deck.

steps.title: Add the front seat cleat
steps.body: Screw the other 2x2 cleat to the inside of the front base, about 1" down from the top edge — the drop recesses the seat deck so cushions sit in the frame instead of sliding off it.

steps.title: Deck the seat
steps.body: Lay the ten 1x4 seat slats across the cleats and screw down with 1-1/2" screws, one per end. Sand everything, finish it exterior-grade, add cushions, and take the first nap in something you built.

images.alt: Modern wood outdoor sofa with reclined slat back and deep cushions
